coredata.dc:title: In situ two-photon fluorescence spectroscopy to precisely reveal intracellular lipid droplet microenvironment via a carbonized polymer dot nanosensor

coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Zitong
Cao, Xiumian
Huo, Zepeng
Xu, Weiqing
Xu, Shuping
coredata.dc:description: Lipid droplets (LDs) are vital dynamic cellular organelles that maintain lipid equilibrium and support biological communication. The polarity of LDs is critical, as their heterogeneity is closely related to pathophysiological developments. To investigate these changes, we developed a polarity-specific two-photon fluorescence (TPF) platform for real-time and precise monitoring of the polarity microenvironment of intracellular LDs with a carbonized polymer dots (CPD) probe. This platform enables the accurate and in situ polarity assessment of a single LD. Using this platform, we identified differences in LD polarity between cancerous and non-cancerous cells, as well as variations in LD polarity during pathophysiological events such as ferroptosis and steatosis. The development of the CPD probe associated with TPF spectroscopy marks a significant advancement in understanding the role of LDs in lipid-associated diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Lipid droplet
Solvatochromism
Polarity heterogeneity
Two-photon fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In situ TPF imaging and spectroscopy for cells
Characterizations and optical properties of CPD
LD-targeted feature and cytotoxicity test of CPD
Solvatochromic characteristics disclosed by TPF spectroscopy
TPF spectroscopy of a single LD stained by CPD
LD microenvironments in different cell lines
LD variation during ferroptosis and steatosis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank Prof. Bai Yang, Jilin University for his helpful discussion. This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China NSFC (Nos. 22373041 and 22173035), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2023YFC3706002), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, Science and Technology Development Program Projects of Jilin Province (20220101046JC), Innovative Research Project of State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials. Supporting information. The Supporting information is available free of charge at Experimental section, TPA properties and measurement of TPA Cross-Section, wash-free imaging feature, co-staining imaging, Erastin and Erastin plus DFO actions on Cells with other organelles, OA action on Cells and tables (PDF).

We thank Prof. Bai Yang, Jilin University for his helpful discussion. This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China NSFC (Nos. 22373041 and 22173035), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2023YFC3706002), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, Science and Technology Development Program Projects of Jilin Province (20220101046JC), Innovative research project of State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, high-resolution transmission electron microscope images (HRTEM) (A), histograms of the particle size distributions (B), FT-IR spectra (C), 1H NMR spectra in dimethyl sulfoxide-d6 (D), XPS survey spectra survey (E), and XPS survey scans of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s (F–H) of the CPD, respectively. (I) UV–vis absorption (depicted in red, left coordinate axis), PL excitation (marked as EX), and emission spectra (depicted in black, right coordinate axis) of the CPD at different excitation wavelengths. All spectra were recorded in ethanol. (J) Normalized PL emission spectra. (K) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CPD in diverse solvents. (L) Quantum yields (QYs) of the CPD in aprotic and protic solvents. (M) The linear fitting curve of the PL emission wavelengths with the solvent parameters ET (30). (N) PL emission spectra of the CPD in 1,4-dioxane with different water contents.

(A) HeLa cells CLSM images co-stained with Hoechst 33342 living nuclear dye, BODIPY 493/503 and CPD. Scale bar: 10 μm. Hoechst: λex = 405 nm; CPD: λex = 561 nm; BODIPY: λex = 488 nm;(B) Two-tunnel scatter diagram (BODIPY 493/503 and CPD) for co-localization. Pearson correlation coefficients (PCC) are PCC(CPD) = 0.944. (C) Plot profile analysis along the white lines (A). (D) Cell viabilities of HeLa cells in the presence of different concentrations (0–50 μg/mL) of the CPD.
(A) Normalized TPF emission of CPD in diverse solvents. (B) The fitting curve of the TPF emission wavelengths with the solvent parameters ET (30).
TPF images of HeLa cells co-stained with Hoechst 33342 and CPD (λex = 790 nm). Scale bar: 10 μm.
(A) The TPF spectra of four single LDs (a–d) in a single cell as marked in a TPF image of a single cell (B). λex = 790 nm. The scale bar in Image (B) is 5 μm. (C) Statistics of the MEW.

(A) TPF images of single-cell field-of-view from six different cell lines and their corresponding in situ TPF spectra. Gaussian fitting is applied to yield the average spectral profiles (solid curves) and the shadows indicate deviation. (λex = 790 nm) (B)The MEWs of various cell lines were counted. (C) Quantitative analysis of the MEWs of various cell lines. *: p < 0.05, **: p < 0.01, ***: p < 0.001. Scale bar: 5 μm.

Single-cell TPF images and corresponding in situ TPF spectra (A) of HeLa cells after they were treated with different Erastin concentrations followed with the CPD staining and their MEW (B). (C) Quantitative analysis of MEWs in Hela cells treated with different Erastin concentrations. Scale bar: 5 μm. (D) TPF spectra and images of HeLa cells after they were stimulated by oleic acid. The zoom-in images from the circled regions are shown in the right panel. (E) The MEW of LDs from OA stimulation processes. (λex = 790 nm) (F) Quantitative analysis of MEWs in Hela cells treated with different OA concentrations. *: p < 0.05, **: p < 0.01, ***: p < 0.001. Scale bar: 10 μm.
The schematic diagram for acquiring TPF spectra.

head.ce:title: In situ two-photon fluorescence spectroscopy to precisely reveal intracellular lipid droplet microenvironment via a carbonized polymer dot nanosensor

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lipid droplets (LDs) are vital dynamic cellular organelles that maintain lipid equilibrium and support biological communication. The polarity of LDs is critical, as their heterogeneity is closely related to pathophysiological developments. To investigate these changes, we developed a polarity-specific two-photon fluorescence (TPF) platform for real-time and precise monitoring of the polarity microenvironment of intracellular LDs with a carbonized polymer dots (CPD) probe. This platform enables the accurate and in situ polarity assessment of a single LD. Using this platform, we identified differences in LD polarity between cancerous and non-cancerous cells, as well as variations in LD polarity during pathophysiological events such as ferroptosis and steatosis. The development of the CPD probe associated with TPF spectroscopy marks a significant advancement in understanding the role of LDs in lipid-associated diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lipid droplets (LDs) are distinct organelles characterized by a spherical form, encapsulating a core of neutral lipids, including sterol esters and triacylglycerols, all enclosed by a single layer of phospholipids [1–3]. Emerging research supports that variations in LDs, such as shifts in number, size, polarity, composition, and spatial arrangement, mirror the metabolic conditions of cells or tissues, thereby playing a pivotal role in diverse cellular processes including metabolism and cell growth [4]. These LDs are pivotal in oncogenesis and thus are recognized as cancer biomarkers [5,6]. In the tumorigenic process, cancerous cells voraciously consume nutrients and accrue substantial amounts of lipids and cholesterol. These metabolites would accumulate and be stored within LDs, altering their polarity. Such changes in LD polarity indicate the dynamic and complex interactions sustaining cellular physiology [7,8]. Notably, deviations in polarity are closely linked with the initiation and progression of diseases, including inflammation and cancer [9,10]. Consequently, monitoring shifts in LD polarity due to lipid accumulation could yield critical insights [11,12]. Given the dynamics and variability of LD polarity influenced by cellular activities, precisely monitoring these fluctuations is crucial for understanding the pathological states tied to abnormal lipid metabolism [13–15].

Solvent chromogenic probes have garnered increased attention in recent years, attributed to their capacity for color variation in response to polarity changes, primarily driven by the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) mechanism [16,17]. These probes expand the utility of fluorescence imaging technology, offering a potent modality for visualizing subcellular organelles and tracking diverse physiological processes with high selectivity, high sensitivity, and exceptional spatial resolution. One application of solvent chromogenic probes is to monitor alterations in subcellular polarity, e.g., intracellular LDs [18–20]. However, the accuracy of LD polarity monitoring is compromised by probe concentration, inherent fluorescence, photobleaching, and unavoidable washing steps. Consequently, there is an imperative need to develop an innovative polarity sensing probe that can provide direct, real-time, and in situ visualization of both the polarity and functional status of intracellular LDs.

Our previous study reported an LD-targeted fluorescent nanoprobe based on carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) prepared through a solvothermal method using N, N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine as a precursor [21,22]. This CPD-based nanoprobe possesses excellent one-photon fluorescence (OPF) and two-photon fluorescence (TPF) properties, as well as a solvatochromic feature derived from its intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) characteristics and D-π-A structure. Notably, this probe enables wash-free, LD-targeted imaging, making it highly valuable for real-time monitoring of intracellular LDs.

Inspired by our previous work, herein, we developed a TPF spectroscopic platform integrated with a standard multiphoton imaging microscope [23], enabling the acquisition of the TPF spectrum of a single LD along with TPF imaging results. TPF imaging offers several advantages over OPF imaging, including improved capability for deeper tissue penetration, reduced photodamage to biological specimens, and decreased interference from background fluorescence [24,25]. Our innovative analytical platform enables monitoring subtle changes in the polarity of LDs in real-time and in situ through the TPF spectroscopy. This method offers higher accuracy in determining LD polarity and provides a more detailed understanding of LD behaviors beyond the traditional filter-based OPF/TPF imaging systems that fail to extract slight differences among LDs, since they record the fluorescence integration across a relatively wider wavelength range of a filter channel. By using this analytical platform paired with the polarity-sensitive CPD probe, we observed various dynamic processes associated with LDs, such as revealing differences in LD polarity between cancerous and healthy cells, as well as changes in cellular LD polarity during ferroptosis and steatosis. Different types of cancer cells have been successfully distinguished from normal cells, enabling potential cancer diagnosis and enhancing its clinical utility in diagnosing LD-related disorders such as steatosis, inflammatory reactions, etc.

A solvothermal method was employed to synthesize CPD, as illustrated in Fig. S1A. A polytetrafluoroethylene-lined reactor was utilized during this procedure, containing 10 mL of ethanol and 40 mg of N, N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine. Subsequently, the mixture underwent heating at 200 °C for 12 h within an autoclave. Upon cooling to ambient temperature, the resulting substance was isolated using column chromatography on silica gel, with dichloromethane and methanol as the mobile phases. Fig. S1B (a–c) shows the isolation process for a red fluorescent component. The obtained CPD was characterized by transmission electron microscopy, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption, ¹H nuclear magnetic resonance, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS). Its quantum yield (QY) and lifetime were also measured. More details about the apparatus are provided in Supporting information. The prepared CPD in ethanol was stored at 4 °C in the dark for further use.

The lipid-responsive feature of the CPD was verified by dissolving it into n-octanol, which can simulate an intracellular lipid-like environment and provide a high polarity environment. As illustrated in Fig. S1B(d), when the n-octanol/water solution was exposed to ultraviolet light, a distinct variation in fluorescence was observed between the two solvents, indicating that our CPD provides the lipid-targeted ability.

The solvatochromic feature of the CPD was testified by measuring the TPF spectra of the CPDs (1.0 mg/mL) in different solvents including petroleum ether, cyclohexane, carbon tetrachloride, toluene, 1,4-dioxane, dichloromethane, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). A fitting curve of the maximum value of the TPF emission wavelength was plotted with the solvent parameters ET (30).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ TPF imaging and spectroscopy for cells
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular imaging was achieved using HeLa cells as a model cell. Inoculated HeLa cells were cultured on glass-bottomed cell culture dishes for 24 h in DMEM media with 10 % FBS (1 × 105 cells/mL, 37 °C, 5 % CO2). After that, they were stimulated with 300 μM of oleic acid for 8 h to induce more LDs in the cytoplasm. CPDs (50 μg/mL) and Hoechst 33342 (10 μg/mL) in DMSO were used for cell staining (20 mm cell dish with 1.0 mL of the DMEM medium) for 10 min. Then, the cells were washed with phosphate buffer saline. Next, they were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for subsequent TPF imaging.

TPF imaging was achieved on a multiphoton laser scanning confocal microscope (FVMPE-RS, Olympus Co. Ltd., Japan) with a high-speed scanning galvanometer. The laser source is a tunable picosecond laser with an excitation wavelength range from 700 nm to 960 nm (picoEmerald, APE, Germany). The optical configuration utilizes a 50 × objective lens with a numerical aperture (NA) of 0.9, supplied by Olympus, which facilitates the excitation and collection of TPF signals with an upright mode.

Scheme 1 shows the basic configuration of the TPF imaging and spectroscopy system. The laser first passes through the scanning galvanometer system, controlling the direction of the beam in the x and y directions, respectively. After that, the laser passes through a dichroic mirror (690 nm, long-pass) and then focuses on the sample surface with an objective lens. The fluorescence signal emitted by the sample is collected by the same objective lens, and since the wavelength of the signal light is shorter than 690 nm, which is in the reflective wavelength range of the dichroic mirror, it is reflected to the horizontal direction and focused on the photomultiplier tube (PMT) detector after the excitation light is filtered out by a short-pass filter to create fluorescence images. A switchable mirror is provided in front of the PMT detector, reflecting the collimated beam in the upward direction, which is coupled into an optical fiber by a lens. The TPF signal is transmitted by the optical fiber to and acquired in situ by a spectrometer (Ocean Optics USB4000, USA). In this case, the integration time for the spectrometer measurements was 10 s, and the number of averages was 5. FV31S-SW software manages all operational aspects of this setup. To record the TPF spectra of a specific region, we zoomed in and captured a frame-selected area from the TPF image using the operation software. Over five spectra were obtained for each trial, and an averaged curve was plotted by Gaussian fitting (solid curves) and the shadows indicate deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and optical properties of CPD
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to analyze the particle size and morphology of CPD. As shown in Fig. 1A, the CPDs dispersed in ethanol exhibit a consistently spherical shape. The average diameter of these particles (Fig. 1B) is 8.5 ± 0.5 nm. Additionally, a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image as an inserted image of Fig. 1A reveals that the lattice stripes on the CPD give a spacing distance of 0.21 nm, indicating the (1, 0, 0) crystal plane in graphitic carbon.

Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis can identify surface functional groups on the CPD. As depicted in Fig. 1C, peaks at 2924 and 2854 cm−1 correspond to C-H groups, and a 1248 cm−1 peak is indicative of C-O groups, both contributing to an enhanced solubility in water. Additionally, the formation of a polyaromatic structure during the CPD preparation is suggested by the evidence of the stretching vibrations of CC and CN bonds at 1626 and 1350 cm−1, respectively. Collectively, the FT-IR data preliminarily proves many carbon and nitrogen-related functional groups in CPDs.

The ¹H NMR spectroscopy elucidates the molecular architecture of CPD. Illustrative data in Fig. 1D demonstrate the presence of aromatic hydrogen resonating between 6 and 10 ppm and aliphatic hydrogens between 1 and 4 ppm. These suggest that the precursors likely underwent a series of transformations, including deamination, dehydrogenation, and dehydration processes during the high-temperature treatment in the solvent, creating nitrogen-doped π-conjugated structures. Such structures are characterized by a plethora of electron-donating (D, e.g., -NR₂) and accepting groups (A, e.g., -C-O) located at the periphery of the CPD. Consequently, this configuration yields a D-π-A type structure, imparting the solvatochromic property of our prepared CPD.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) analysis provides detailed insights into the surface chemistry of the CPD. Fig. 1E displays the characteristic peaks at 284, 400, and 533 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. Detailed examinations of the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 1F–H) reveal that the C1s peak could be deconvoluted into three distinct components: sp²-bonded carbon at 284.8 eV, sp³-bonded carbon interacting with nitrogen at 285.91 eV, and carbonyl groups at 288.10 eV. Similarly, the N1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 399.44 and 400.15 eV, which are indicative of amino and pyrrole nitrogen forms, respectively. The O1s profile can be differentiated into the peaks at 531.73 and 533.78 eV, associated with CO single bonds and C-O-C or C-OH functionalities, respectively. Quantitative analyses conducted on the elemental and functional group proportions are documented in Table S1–S4. Moreover, CPD manifests as an aromatic compound due to hybridization involving sp² and sp³ orbitals, which is further modified by the presence of polar groups adorned with hydroxyl functionalities (oxygen and nitrogen groups), thereby enhancing its solubility in polar solvents. In summary, the above results confirm the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and carbonyl functionalities, substantiating the successful synthesis of the CPD.

Fig. 1I presents the UV–visible absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra for CPD dissolved in ethanol, demonstrating an excitation wavelength-independent feature. The peak of fluorescence emission is observed at 630 nm. Additionally, a minimal overlap between the absorption spectrum and the emission bands indicates a negligible self-absorption effect, predicting that our CPD allows enhanced fluorescence efficiency.

Subsequent investigations of the fluorescence and absorption characteristics of CPD across various solvents have been detailed in Fig. 1J–K, highlighting its response to solvent environments. Notably, the absorption and fluorescence peaks for CPD exhibit a progressive red shift as solvent polarity increases. This shift is particularly pronounced when comparing the emission wavelengths in polar (water) and non-polar (toluene) solvents, which enhances selective LD imaging applications. The sensitivity of CPD to solvent polarity stems from its molecular structure. FT-IR characterization (Fig. 1C) suggests that CPD contains functional groups, including hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl, alongside a conjugated system. The formation of a D-π-A structure is conducive to ICT. The environments impacting ICT would lead to its luminescence change, manifesting environmentally responsive behaviors. To display this impact, we measured the QY and lifetime (Table S6) of the CPD in different solvents. It can be observed that CPD shows a decrease in QY in highly polar solvents (Fig. 1L) due to the solvation effect, e.g., the QY value of CPD is 1.67 % in water but 83.56 % in DMSO. Such a marked disparity brings our CPD with an LD-targeted capability. The correlation between the maximum emission wavelength (MEW) and the solvent polarity parameter [ET (30)] was determined. ET (30) displays the polarity of a solvent, which is defined as the molar absorption energy of 2,6-diphenyl-4-(2,4,6-triphenyl-1-pyridinio)phenolate measured in a certain solvent [26–28]. We listed the ET (30) values of solvents we used in Table S5 in Supporting information. As shown in Fig. 1M, the correlation of MEW and ET (30) shows a linear relation, with a correlation coefficient (R2) value of 0.961. The solvatochromic characteristics were further explored by recording a series of photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CPD in 1,4-dioxane/water mixed solvents. As demonstrated in Fig. 1N, the fluorescence intensity of CPD declines markedly as the water content increases, coupled with a gradual red shift in the emission wavelength. These findings underline the potential of CPD for intracellular LD microenvironment sensing.

As mentioned above, the edge-passivated amino group and the oxygen-containing functional group introduced by ethanol form a D-π-A-like structure, which not only promotes the sensitive response of CPDs to the solvent polarity, but also confers the TPA properties of CPDs. In this study, the TPF properties of CPDs were investigated. It is well known that for multiphoton-induced fluorescence processes, the fluorescence intensity depends on the excitation intensity (Iex), and the n-order relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the laser power can determine the number of photons involved in its up-conversion process. Therefore, the CPDs in ethanol were irradiated with a tunable picosecond pulsed laser (790 nm). Different excitation intensities were adjusted to collect their up-conversion fluorescence spectra, and the results are shown in Fig. S2(A–B). It can be observed that the up-conversion fluorescence intensity increases with increasing laser power. The emission wavelength of (637 nm) is close to the OPF emission peak, and the logarithm of the peak area integral of the upconverted excitation fluorescence bands shows a good linear relationship with the logarithm of the laser intensity, with a slope of 1.978. This result proves that our CPD displays a typical two-photon absorption (TPA) effect, and its TPA cross-section (σ) (Fig. S2C) has a maximum of about 40 GM, making it suitable for high-performance TPF imaging and sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LD-targeted feature and cytotoxicity test of CPD
ce:section.ce:para: The LD-targeted feature of our CPD was testified by confocal fluorescence imaging. Fig. 2A–C show a significant overlap in the fluorescence emitted by CPD and the BODIPY, confirming the co-localization of CPD with LDs. A Pearson co-localization coefficient (PCC): PCC(CPD) equals 0.944, indicating its effective LD-targeting properties (Fig. 2B). Correlation analysis of fluorescence intensity and pixel distribution shows high similarity, as illustrated in Fig. 2C. The data show that this CPD has the ability to target LD effectively.

The WST-1 assay was used to assess the cytotoxicity of CPD. The WST-1 results (Fig. 2D) show they maintained a survival rate exceeding 85 %, suggesting that our CPD displays excellent biocompatibility and minimal cytotoxicity.

Our CPD enables wash-free imaging. We compared LD imaging with no PBS washing, as shown in Fig. S3A. It can be observed that these CPDs exhibit a sufficiently high imaging contrast. After repeated washing with PBS, the fluorescent strong signal remained in the cells, indicating that water-free washing imaging can be achieved by using this novel CPD-based nanoprobe (Fig. S3B–C).

To further demonstrate the high specificity of CPD for LDs, we co-stained cellular organelles using commercial dyes. Probes specific for mitochondria and lysosomes emit green fluorescence, which differs from the red fluorescence channel of CPD, as shown in Fig. S4A–B. The green fluorescence from mitochondria and lysosomes shows no signal interference, confirming the high specificity and compatibility of CPD with intracellular LDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvatochromic characteristics disclosed by TPF spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: In the synthesis of CPD, the introduction of ethanol facilitated the creation of edge-passivating amino groups and oxygen-containing functionalities, establishing a D-π-A-like architecture [29]. This configuration endows it with TPF capabilities while enhancing its responsive sensitivity to solvent polarity.

Fig. 3 elucidates the solvatochromic characteristics of our CPD, revealed by the TPF spectroscopy using the setup as illustrated in Scheme 1. Notably, the peak absorption wavelength of CPD for TPF demonstrates a consistent red shift correlating with the increase in solvent polarity, which shows the same trend as the observation of OPF (Fig. 1I). An analysis of the data presented in Table S5 shows a linear relationship between the MEW and the solvent polarity parameter [ET (30)], with an R2 of 0.969 (Fig. 3B). These data demonstrate that the solvatochromic characteristic of CPD is remarkable. By combining the TPF microscope and spectrometer setup, we can accurately evaluate solvent polarity and the microenvironment polarity of cellular LDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TPF spectroscopy of a single LD stained by CPD
ce:section.ce:para: The TPF characteristic of CPD was exploited to investigate the LD microenvironment within cells. First, TPF fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells stimulated with 300 μM of oleic acid for 8 h, co-stained with CPD and Hoechst 33342, was performed, as depicted in Fig. 4. CPDs can image intracellular LDs with high quality and specificity at near-infrared laser (790 nm), demonstrating the remarkable labeling ability of CPDs for LDs.

Next, TPF spectra were recorded to illustrate the polarity of LDs more intuitively. We reduced the magnification of the objective lens and narrowed the range of the acquisition field to restrict the laser scanning region on an individual LD. We obtained the spectra of several droplets ( Fig. 5A) from the regions marked in Fig. 5B, and the data for the MEW are shown in Fig. 5C. Noticeably, the emission peak wavelength differs by a few nanometers between the LDs closer to and further from the nucleus. It is well known that LDs are synthesized within the endoplasmic reticulum membrane and accumulate in the middle of the phospholipid bilayer. As neutral lipids accumulate, the nascent droplets separate from the endoplasmic reticulum membrane as ‘buds’ to become mature LDs [30,31]. Our TPF results reveal that the polarity of LDs away from the nucleus was slightly higher than that of those near the nucleus, which confirms that the polarity of the nascent LDs was slightly lower than that of the original LDs. From these data, we can conclude that this TPF spectroscopy system enables the determination of the intracellular LD microenvironment at a single LD level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LD microenvironments in different cell lines
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular polarity, crucial for numerous metabolic reactions, is often compromised in pathological states. Research indicates substantial variations in polarity across different cell types, with normal cells demonstrating more pronounced polarity of LDs compared to their cancerous counterparts [32,33]. Given these disparities, accurately determining LD polarity is essential for distinguishing between malignant and benign cells. Here, we employed TPF spectroscopy with the help of the CPD-based LD-specific nanoprobe to assess the polarity of LDs in various cell lines. Fig. 6A reveals that LDs in three cancerous cell lines (MDA-MB-231, HeLa, and MCF-7) emit stronger fluorescence compared to those in three non-cancerous lines (L-929, H8, and 293T), suggesting a decrease in LD polarity in cancer cells. Five or more TPF spectra collected from single cells were recorded rapidly, and Gaussian fitting was applied to yield the average emission profiles for each cell type, as depicted in Fig. 6A. Comparisons of emission wavelengths from LDs across the cell lines are displayed in Fig. 6B, indicating shorter wavelengths for cancer cell LDs relative to normal cells. Fig. 6C shows significant differences between tumor and normal cell lines. Utilizing the linear relationship between MEW and ET (30) established in Fig. 3B, we calculated the polarity parameters of LDs for each cell line, summarized in Table S7. This approach, which contrasts with traditional single-emission fluorescence intensity methods, provides a more accurate determination and detailed information of LD polarity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LD variation during ferroptosis and steatosis
ce:section.ce:para: Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent non-apoptotic cell death, results from extensive lipid peroxide accumulation, with LD polarity changes integral to this mechanism. To investigate this, we analyzed LD polarity alterations during Ferroptosis using TPF spectroscopy. Erastin, known to induce Ferroptosis, depletes glutathione and accumulates reactive oxygen species and lipid peroxides by inhibiting cystine uptake [34,35]. Initially, we evaluated the Erastin effect on Ferroptosis with a Liperfluo Cell Lipid Peroxide (LPO) Assay Kit since this kit can detect LPO (a marker of Ferroptosis) in the green fluorescence channel. Five petri dish sets with varying Erastin concentrations (0, 5, 10, 25, and 50 μM) were tested, and the fluorescence images of Erastin acted cells were taken via CLSM. As indicated in Fig. S5, a progressive rise in green fluorescence with the Erastin level increasing was observed, confirming that the extent of cellular ferroptosis is positively related to the Erastin level.

Then, the TPF images and spectra of cells after Erastin action were collected at a single cell level. To ensure the accuracy of the results, we selected five or more single-cell fields and their LD polarity spectra. These spectra were treated via Gaussian fitting to obtain the average profiles ( Fig. 7A). The TPF imaging data display that higher Erastin concentrations correlated with fewer LDs and diminished fluorescence brightness, indicating that the addition of Erastin caused the cells to undergo iron death, and apoptosis was accompanied by a substantial reduction in the number of lipid droplets. The TPF spectra reveal that the emission wavelength of LDs (Fig. 7B) gives a redshift, indicating increased polarity with Erastin concentration during Ferroptosis. Fig. 7C show significant differences between polarity of HeLa cells at different Erastin concentrations. Excessive accumulation of lipid hydroperoxides triggered a rapid reaction in LDs, leading to changes in the structure and polarity of LDs. These results confirm that the Ferroptosis process can lead to an increase in LD polarity.

Deferoxamine (DFO) is a recognized Ferroptosis inhibitor that hinders the buildup of lipid peroxides and lipid-reactive oxygen species in living cells. We subsequently examined the alterations about lipid peroxides within live HeLa cells exposed to CPD, Erastin, and DFO concurrently. As depicted in Fig. S6, cells treated with 25 μM of Erastin for 6 h exhibited strong fluorescence, indicating highly fluorescent lipid peroxides. In contrast, when HeLa cells were incubated with 25 μM of Erastin and 50 μM of DFO for the same duration, fluorescence was nearly absent, demonstrating the inhibitory effect of DFO on Ferroptosis. Spectral data were then collected for untreated cells, cells treated with 25 μM of Erastin, and cells treated with 25 μM of Erastin and 50 μM of DFO, and then highlighted by CPD, as shown in Fig. S7. These results reveal that the LD polarity remains nearly unchanged (λTPF max = 569.43 nm) after adding the Ferroptosis inhibitor. For the trial with Erastin but without DFO, the λTPF max arrives at 593.51 nm. Consequently, CPD combined with TPF spectroscopy is an effective tool for monitoring Erastin-induced polarity changes in LDs. The ability of CPD to track LD polarity during in situ Ferroptosis suggests that it offers more sensitive biological applications than conventional Ferroptosis detection kits.

The intracellular accumulation of triglycerides, known as neutral fats, leads to Steatosis. LDs, which are dynamic organelles essential for lipid metabolism, contain a core of LDs consisting of triglycerides (TGs) and certain cholesteryl esters [36]. Consequently, Steatosis significantly alters the number and size of LDs and affects their polarity. OA is a nutrient that is readily absorbed by cells and can induce cellular Steatosis [37–39]. Research indicates that OA reduces the polarity of intracellular LDs while promoting the production of large quantities of endogenous LDs [40–42]. To explore the Steatosis effect, the in situ TPF images and spectra of the cells stimulated with OA were recorded, averaged, and fitted using Gaussian functions. As illustrated in Fig. 7D, there is a clear increase in the number, size, and area of LDs, along with a significant rise in fluorescence intensity. (Fig. S8). Analysis of the emission wavelength variations at different OA exposure times (Fig. 7E) reveals that LD polarity decreases as the number of LDs increases during Steatosis, consistent with previous studies [43,44]. Fig. 7F shows significant differences between the polarity of HeLa cells at different OA concentrations. These data demonstrate that our TPF spectroscopic platform is feasible for real-time dynamic monitoring of LD polarity.

In summary, we developed an innovative platform to evaluate LD polarity via in situ TPF imaging and spectroscopy. This technique offers the advantages of real-time, in situ, and high-fidelity dynamic monitoring of LD polarity alterations in cells. Employing this method, not only did we detect differences in lipid droplet polarity between cancer cells and normal cells, and we successfully detected dynamic changes in LD polarity during the Ferroptosis and Steatosis processes. Most importantly, we detected the polarity of single lipid droplets in cells, greatly increasing the detection limit. This research enhances the understanding of the correlation between LD polarity and the pathogenesis of various diseases. It provides a reference for designing novel LD-targeting probes, in situ fluorescence spectroscopic imaging, and clinical diagnosis of LD-related disorders. In the future, we will continue to develop the potential applications of TPF technology in other pathology models, such as rapid classification of tumor malignancy by TPF in combination with machine learning, as well as diagnostics for the clinic to provide rapid pathology on tissue sections, to broaden its application in cellular imaging and clinical diagnostics.

Zitong Yu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zepeng Huo: Methodology, Data curation. Xiumian Cao: Methodology, Data curation. Weiqing Xu: Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Shuping Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Prof. Bai Yang, Jilin University for his helpful discussion. This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China NSFC (Nos. 22373041 and 22173035), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2023YFC3706002), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, Science and Technology Development Program Projects of Jilin Province (20220101046JC), Innovative Research Project of State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Supporting information. The Supporting information is available free of charge at Experimental section, TPA properties and measurement of TPA Cross-Section, wash-free imaging feature, co-staining imaging, Erastin and Erastin plus DFO actions on Cells with other organelles, OA action on Cells and tables (PDF).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136900.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zitong Yu is a Ph.D. student in the State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University. Her research focuses on the synthesis of carbon dots and imaging applications.
Xiumian Cao is a Ph.D. student in College of Physics, Jilin University. His research interests focus on the construction and application of spectroscopic instruments.

Zepeng Huo is a Ph.D. student in the State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University. His research interests focus on the synthesis and application of carbon dots.

Weiqing Xu has been a Professor of Physical Chemistry at Jilin University since 2000. His research interests are SERS technology, high-pressure spectroscopy, nanophotonic and plasmon-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, molecular assembly, and the development of analytical instruments.

Shuping Xu received her Ph.D. in Physical Chemistry from Jilin University in 2006. Since then, she has worked in the State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials at Jilin University. She is a Professor in Analytical Chemistry with research interests involving nanomaterials, SERS spectroscopy, and biosensing/bioimaging.
